cards.target: ちょっと まってください。
cards.notes: Possibly premature — explicitly taught later in Lesson 19: Making a Request: Please Give Her My Regards (explicitly re-taught as a dedicated KEY SENTENCES entry ("Please wait a moment."))

cards.target: もう いちど おねがいします。
cards.notes: Possibly premature — explicitly taught later in Lesson 2: Possession: Whose Pen Is This? (explicitly re-taught as a dedicated VOCABULARY entry ("One more time, please.")) | Possibly incorrect romanization — incorrectly splits 'onegaishimasu' into 'onegai shi masu' — target shows no space within this word
Particle marking belonging or affiliation, e.g. company + の + person (see GRAMMAR 3)

Suffix — attaches to the end of a noun to mean 'is/am/are' (written 〜です in the source). Example: (わたしは) スミスです。 — '[I am] Smith.' Attaches directly after the noun with no space, and simply states what someone or something is.
Placed at the end of a sentence to form a question (see GRAMMAR 2)
Follows noun 1, marking it as the sentence topic; similar to 'as for' in English. Example: スミスさんは アメリカじんです。 — 'As for Smith-san, (he) is American.' (i.e. 'Smith-san is American.')
Possibly incorrect romanization — should be 'iie' (three separate i sounds), not 'īe' (single long i); the two い characters are distinct syllables

お is not a word on its own — it's a short prefix stuck onto the FRONT of certain nouns to make them sound more polite/respectful. It's used almost entirely for things belonging to the person you're talking to (or a third person), not your own things — e.g. お + くに ('country') → おくに ('your country'), heard in this chapter's own dialogue: スミスさん、おくには どちらですか。 ('Smith-san, what country are you from?'). Think of it like the difference between 'your country' (plain) and a more deferential way of asking about someone else's country in English — お signals that same extra politeness, just attached directly to the front of the word instead of being a separate word.

cards.target: たなかさんを おねがいします
cards.pronunciation: ta naka san o onegai shi masu
cards.notes: Placeholder filled with 'たなかさん', the exact example given in the chapter's own NOTES section — used when asking a receptionist to summon someone (source shows only '〜を おねがいします')
Affirmative counterpart to じゃありません below — see GRAMMAR 2 (already introduced in Lesson 1, repeated here to sit alongside its negative form)
Negative form of です (source shows 〜じゃありません); じゃ is more informal than では but otherwise the same

Past affirmative form of です (source shows 〜でした) | Possibly incorrect romanization — でした should be romanized as 'deshita' (one unit) or 'de shita', not 'deshi ta' which incorrectly combines the first two mora

cards.target: じゃありませんでした
cards.pronunciation: ja ari mase n deshi ta
cards.notes: Past negative form of です (source shows 〜じゃありませんでした); ではありませんでした is the less-informal equivalent | Possibly incorrect romanization — the 'deshi ta' portion at the end incorrectly splits でした; should be 'deshita' or 'de shita'
Possibly incorrect romanization — The word should be romanized as 'jūsho', not split as 'jū sho'
Possibly incorrect romanization — Spacing breaks the word incorrectly; should be 'denwa bangou' not 'denwa ban go u'
